steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills an opening phrase before the main clause "it has since been translated into other languages." An introductory phrase cannot contain its own finite verb — it must use a non-finite form (like a past participle) to modify the subject of the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'is written': A present-tense finite verb. This creates a second independent clause ("Originally is written in ancient Greek") with no conjunction joining it to the main clause — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'had been written': A past perfect finite verb. Same problem as A: it forms a full verb phrase that turns the opening into an independent clause, producing a comma splice or a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — 'written': A past participle used as a modifier. "Originally written in ancient Greek" is a participial phrase that correctly introduces the main clause — no finite verb, no structural problem. ✓
  • Choice D — 'was written': A simple past finite verb. Again produces an independent clause ("Originally was written in ancient Greek"), which cannot be joined to the main clause by a comma alone. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
